project: SafeStep: Verified Deployment Plans with Rollback Safety Envelopes

deployment_safety_literature.citation: R. Di Cosmo, S. Zacchiroli, G. Zavattaro. Aeolus: A component model for the cloud. ESOP 2014, LNCS 8410, pp. 94-114.
deployment_safety_literature.relevance: Establishes NP/PSPACE-hardness of cloud configuration synthesis; SafeStep extends this to sequential deployment planning with rollback

deployment_safety_literature.citation: R. Di Cosmo, M. Lienhardt, J. Mauro, S. Zacchiroli, G. Zavattaro. Zephyrus: Automatic configuration of cloud applications. FACS 2015, LNCS 8997, pp. 1-17.
deployment_safety_literature.relevance: MiniZinc-based configuration optimization; SafeStep addresses sequential planning (path existence) rather than single-point configuration

deployment_safety_literature.citation: A. Biere, A. Cimatti, E. Clarke, O. Strichman, Y. Zhu. Bounded model checking. Advances in Computers, 58:117-148, 2003.
deployment_safety_literature.relevance: Foundation for SafeStep's BMC-based planning engine

deployment_safety_literature.citation: E. Clarke, O. Grumberg, S. Jha, Y. Lu, H. Veith. Counterexample-guided abstraction refinement. CAV 2000, LNCS 1855, pp. 154-169.
deployment_safety_literature.relevance: Foundation for SafeStep's CEGAR loop between CaDiCaL and Z3

deployment_safety_literature.citation: M. Reitblatt, N. Foster, J. Rexford, C. Schlesinger, D. Walker. Abstractions for network update. SIGCOMM 2012, pp. 323-334.
deployment_safety_literature.relevance: Consistent network updates (per-packet/per-flow consistency); SafeStep adapts this consistency notion to multi-service deployment

deployment_safety_literature.citation: H. Kautz, B. Selman. Planning as satisfiability. ECAI 1992, pp. 359-363.
deployment_safety_literature.relevance: Pioneered SAT-based planning; SafeStep extends with interval-compressed domain-specific encoding

deployment_safety_literature.citation: B. Burns, B. Grant, D. Oppenheimer, E. Brewer, J. Wilkes. Borg, Omega, and Kubernetes. ACM Queue, 14(1):70-93, 2016.
deployment_safety_literature.relevance: Foundational Kubernetes architecture paper; SafeStep targets this deployment ecosystem

deployment_safety_literature.citation: G. Schermann, J. Cito, P. Leitner, U. Zdun, H.C. Gall. An empirical study on principles and practices of continuous delivery and deployment. PeerJ Computer Science, 4:e131, 2018.
deployment_safety_literature.relevance: Empirical study of CD practices and challenges; motivates need for formal deployment verification

deployment_safety_literature.citation: A. Basiri, N. Behnam, R. de Rooij, L. Hochstein, L. Kosewski, J. Reynolds, C. Rosenthal. Chaos Engineering. IEEE Software, 33(3):35-41, 2016.
deployment_safety_literature.relevance: Netflix chaos engineering approach to deployment resilience; SafeStep provides proactive formal guarantees vs reactive testing

deployment_safety_literature.citation: B. Beyer, C. Jones, J. Petoff, N.R. Murphy. Site Reliability Engineering: How Google Runs Production Systems. O'Reilly Media, 2016.
deployment_safety_literature.relevance: Documents deployment failure patterns at Google scale; motivates rollback safety envelope concept

deployment_safety_literature.citation: X. Zhou, X. Peng, T. Xie, J. Sun, C. Ji, D. Liu, Q. Xiang, C. He. Benchmarking microservice systems for software engineering research. ICSE-C 2018, pp. 323-324.
deployment_safety_literature.relevance: Microservice topology statistics used to calibrate SafeStep's synthetic benchmarks

deployment_safety_literature.citation: D. Taibi, V. Lenarduzzi, C. Pahl. Processes, motivations, and issues for migrating to microservices architectures: An empirical investigation. IEEE Cloud Computing, 4(5):22-32, 2017.
deployment_safety_literature.relevance: Documents deployment challenges in microservice migration; validates SafeStep's problem space

deployment_safety_literature.citation: A. Rahman, E. Helms, L. Williams, C. Parnin. Characterizing defective configuration scripts used for continuous deployment. ICST 2016, pp. 34-45.
deployment_safety_literature.relevance: Empirical analysis of configuration defects causing deployment failures

deployment_safety_literature.citation: T. Leesatapornwongsa, J.F. Lukman, S. Lu, H.S. Gunawi. TaxDC: A taxonomy of non-deterministic concurrency bugs in datacenter distributed systems. ASPLOS 2016, pp. 517-530.
deployment_safety_literature.relevance: Taxonomy of datacenter system bugs including deployment-related failures

deployment_safety_literature.citation: A. Tarvo, P.F. Sweeney, N. Mitchell, V.R. Rajan, M. Arnold, I. Baldini. CanaryAdvisor: A statistical-based tool for canary testing. ISSTA 2015, pp. 418-422.
deployment_safety_literature.relevance: Canary testing approach; SafeStep provides formal guarantees vs statistical canary analysis

industry_tools.name: Netflix Spinnaker
industry_tools.citation: Netflix. Spinnaker: Multi-cloud continuous delivery platform. https://spinnaker.io/, 2024.
industry_tools.comparison: Pipeline-based multi-cloud deployment; supports canary analysis via Kayenta. No formal verification, no rollback safety envelopes, no cross-service constraint reasoning. Manual pipeline construction required.

industry_tools.name: Argo Rollouts
industry_tools.citation: Argo Project. Argo Rollouts: Progressive delivery for Kubernetes. https://argoproj.github.io/rollouts/, 2024.
industry_tools.comparison: Canary and blue-green deployment strategies for Kubernetes. Single-service scope only; metric-based (not formal); no cross-service ordering constraints; no stuck-configuration detection; cannot reason about multi-step cascading failures.

industry_tools.name: Flagger
industry_tools.citation: Flagger. Progressive delivery Kubernetes operator. https://flagger.app/, 2024.
industry_tools.comparison: CNCF progressive delivery operator. Similar limitations to Argo Rollouts: single-service canary/blue-green, metric-based decisions, no formal cross-service safety proofs.

industry_tools.name: AWS CodeDeploy
industry_tools.citation: Amazon Web Services. AWS CodeDeploy User Guide. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codedeploy/, 2024.
industry_tools.comparison: AWS deployment service for EC2/ECS/Lambda. Supports rolling and blue-green. AWS-only, no cross-service coordination, no formal safety verification, no rollback envelope computation.

industry_tools.name: Harness.io
industry_tools.comparison: CI/CD platform with ML-based deployment verification. Proprietary, ML-based (not formal proofs), no rollback safety envelopes, no stuck-configuration witnesses.

industry_tools.name: Google Cloud Deploy
industry_tools.citation: Google. Google Cloud Deploy documentation. https://cloud.google.com/deploy/docs, 2024.
industry_tools.comparison: Managed CD service for GKE. GCP-only, linear pipeline model, no formal multi-service safety analysis.

industry_tools.name: Helm
industry_tools.citation: The Helm Authors. Helm: The package manager for Kubernetes. https://helm.sh/, 2024.
industry_tools.comparison: Package manager for K8s; handles single-chart deployment and basic rollback. No cross-service planning, no safety verification, no rollback envelope.

industry_tools.name: ArgoCD
industry_tools.citation: Argo Project. Argo CD: Declarative continuous delivery for Kubernetes. https://argoproj.github.io/cd/, 2024.
industry_tools.comparison: GitOps-based CD. Sync waves provide ordering but no safety verification. SafeStep generates ArgoCD-compatible output.

industry_tools.name: Flux
industry_tools.citation: Flux Project. Flux: The GitOps toolkit for Kubernetes. https://fluxcd.io/, 2024.
industry_tools.comparison: GitOps toolkit. Dependencies provide ordering but no formal safety proofs. SafeStep generates Flux-compatible output.

industry_tools.name: Keptn
industry_tools.citation: Keptn Project. Keptn: Cloud-native application lifecycle orchestration. https://keptn.sh/, 2024.
industry_tools.comparison: CNCF cloud-native lifecycle orchestration with SLO-driven quality gates. Best observational cross-service metrics correlation among surveyed tools (46% failure detection). However, evaluations are reactive (post-deployment), cannot reason about version compatibility state space, and cannot compute rollback envelopes or detect stuck configurations.

industry_tools.name: LaunchDarkly
industry_tools.comparison: Feature flag-based gradual rollouts with percentage-based targeting and instant kill switches. Operates at feature level, not service version level; cannot reason about inter-service version compatibility, database schema dependencies, or multi-service deployment state space. Provides instant feature-level rollback but cannot revert version-level changes.

industry_tools.name: Istio
industry_tools.citation: Istio Authors. Istio: Connect, secure, control, and observe services. https://istio.io/, 2024.
industry_tools.comparison: Service mesh providing fine-grained traffic splitting for canary deployments via VirtualService and DestinationRule. Finest-grained traffic control among surveyed tools but no deployment orchestration or safety analysis. Per-service traffic splitting with no cross-service coordination; cannot reason about whether traffic split configurations create cross-service incompatibilities.

incident_reports.citation: Amazon Web Services. Summary of the Amazon S3 service disruption in the Northern Virginia (US-EAST-1) region. https://aws.amazon.com/message/41926/, February 2017.
incident_reports.relevance: Human error in manual deployment procedure without automated safety checks caused cascading S3 failure

incident_reports.citation: Google Cloud. Google Cloud SQL incident report, June 2022. https://status.cloud.google.com/incidents/, 2022.
incident_reports.relevance: Multi-step configuration rollback entered stuck state: intermediate configuration where neither forward nor backward progress was possible. Directly motivates rollback safety envelopes.

incident_reports.citation: J. Graham-Cumming. Details of the Cloudflare outage on July 2, 2019. https://blog.cloudflare.com/details-of-the-cloudflare-outage-on-july-2-2019/, 2019.
incident_reports.relevance: Deployment of faulty rule caused global outage; rapid rollback was possible only by luck, not formal analysis

incident_reports.citation: Amazon Web Services. Summary of the Amazon Kinesis event in the Northern Virginia (US-EAST-1) region. https://aws.amazon.com/message/11201/, November 2020.
incident_reports.relevance: Capacity scaling operation exceeded OS thread limits causing cascading failure; deployment plan had no formal model of parameter interactions

claims.claim: Safe deployment plan existence reduces to path existence in the safe subgraph G[Safe] of the version-product graph

evidence.theorem: Theorem 1 (Problem Characterization) in tool_paper.tex Appendix A
evidence.proof_sketch: Direct encoding: a deployment plan is a sequence of single-service upgrades, which is exactly a walk in G. Safety requires all intermediate states satisfy constraints, restricting to G[Safe]. Plan existence is equivalent to s-t connectivity in G[Safe].
evidence.prior_art: Parallels Aeolus [dicosmo-aeolus-2014] configuration synthesis, but extends to sequential planning with rollback

claims.claim: Under downward-closed compatibility, every safe plan has a monotone equivalent of equal or shorter length (Monotone Sufficiency)

evidence.theorem: Theorem 1 (Monotone Sufficiency) in tool_paper.tex Section 3 and Appendix A.1
evidence.proof_sketch: Exchange argument. Given a plan with a downgrade step (service i from v to v' < v), construct an alternative by deleting the downgrade and subsequent re-upgrades. Downward closure ensures intermediate states remain safe. Plan length decreases by ≥2 per eliminated downgrade. Iterate until no downgrades remain.

claims.claim: Interval encoding compresses compatibility predicates from O(L²) to O(log²L) clauses per service pair

evidence.theorem: Theorem 2 (Interval Encoding Compression) in tool_paper.tex Section 3 and Appendix A.2
evidence.proof_sketch: Binary-encode version indices using ⌈log₂ L⌉ bits. The interval predicate w ∈ [lo, hi] becomes a pair of bit-vector comparisons, each requiring O(log L) clauses via standard binary adder encoding. Total per pair: O(log|Vᵢ| · log|Vⱼ|).
evidence.baseline: Naive quadratic encoding: O(|Vᵢ| × |Vⱼ|) explicit clauses per pair

claims.claim: BMC completeness threshold under monotonicity is k* = Σᵢ(goalᵢ − startᵢ), making SafeStep a complete decision procedure

evidence.theorem: Theorem 3 (BMC Completeness Bound) in tool_paper.tex Section 3 and Appendix A.3
evidence.proof_sketch: Corollary of Monotone Sufficiency. In a monotone plan, each service advances at most goalᵢ − startᵢ steps. Total plan length is at most Σᵢ(goalᵢ − startᵢ) = k*. If no monotone plan of length ≤ k* exists, no monotone plan exists at all.

claims.claim: Treewidth-based DP decides plan existence in O(n · L^{2(w+1)}) time for service dependency graphs of treewidth w

evidence.proof_sketch: Standard bag-based DP on a nice tree decomposition. Each bag of size w+1 has L^{w+1} possible partial assignments. Combining two bags requires comparing all pairs: O(L^{2(w+1)}). Total: n bags × O(L^{2(w+1)}) per bag.
evidence.limitation: Feasible only for treewidth ≤ 3, L ≤ 15. For tw ≥ 4, SAT/BMC is the primary solver.

claims.claim: The CEGAR loop between CaDiCaL and Z3 is sound and refutation-complete, terminating in at most 2^R iterations

evidence.theorem: Theorem 5 (CEGAR Loop Soundness) in tool_paper.tex Section 3
evidence.proof_sketch: Standard CEGAR soundness: each blocking clause eliminates ≥1 candidate. Finite search space (bounded by BMC encoding) guarantees termination. Soundness: Z3 correctly verifies QF_LIA. Refutation-completeness: blocking clauses precisely encode Z3-refuted candidates.

claims.claim: The adversary budget bound theorem provides principled k-robustness scope via one-sided concentration inequality over oracle confidence

evidence.theorem: Theorem 6 (Adversary Budget Bound) in tool_paper.tex Appendix A
evidence.proof_sketch: If each oracle judgment has independent error probability pₑ(i,j,v,w), set k = ⌈Σpₑ + z_α·√(Σpₑ(1−pₑ))⌉. By Hoeffding's inequality, the probability of >k errors is ≤ α. Verifying plan safety under all (red_count choose k) perturbations certifies safety with probability ≥ 1−α.
evidence.limitation: Independence assumption is unrealistic for correlated schema-analysis errors.

claims.claim: Replica symmetry reduction collapses per-service state space from L^r to O(L²) by representing replica configurations as (old_count, new_count) pairs

evidence.theorem: Proposition B (Replica Symmetry Reduction) in tool_paper.tex
evidence.proof_sketch: Under symmetric replica treatment, tracking individual pod versions is redundant. A replica set is fully characterized by how many pods run each version. For two-version rolling updates, the pair (old_count, new_count) with old+new=r suffices, giving O(r) ≤ O(L²) states instead of L^r.

claims.claim: Interval compression reduces clause count from ~2 billion to ~9.3 million at production scale (n≈50, L≈20, k≈200)

evidence.baseline: Naive quadratic encoding produces O(n²·L²·k) clauses

claims.claim: SafeStep computes rollback safety envelopes via bidirectional reachability analysis

evidence.description: For each state on the synthesized plan, forward reachability to target and backward reachability to start are checked. A state is in the envelope iff both hold. Points of no return are states where backward reachability fails.

claims.claim: Stuck-configuration witnesses provide minimal UNSAT-core-based explanations of why rollback is blocked

evidence.description: When backward reachability fails, the system extracts a minimal subset of cross-service constraints that block retreat, giving operators actionable explanations.

claims.claim: k-robustness checking verifies plan safety under all (red_count choose k) perturbations for k∈{1,2}, requiring at most 55 additional SAT calls

evidence.calculation: For k=1: ≤10 red-tagged constraints → 10 SAT calls. For k=2: C(10,2) = 45 calls. Total: ≤55 calls at ~0.1-1s each ≤ 55 seconds.

claims.claim: Schema compatibility oracle classifies changes as breaking/non-breaking with confidence coloring (green/yellow/red)

evidence.description: Every constraint is tagged with provenance-based confidence: green (structural evidence like field removal), yellow (medium evidence like deprecation), red (no evidence, assumed compatible by default).

claims.claim: SafeStep integrates with Helm, Kustomize, ArgoCD, and Flux for Kubernetes-native operation
Safe deployment planning is PSPACE-hard in general, NP-complete under monotonicity

evidence.proof_sketch: General case: reachability in the exponentially large implicit version-product graph is PSPACE-hard, paralleling Aeolus's result for configuration synthesis (Di Cosmo et al., ESOP 2014). Under monotonicity: plan length is polynomially bounded by k* = Σ(goalᵢ − startᵢ), so plan existence is in NP (guess the plan, verify in polynomial time). NP-hardness follows from reduction of 3-SAT to version compatibility constraints.

claims.claim: The rollback safety envelope is a new operational primitive not provided by any existing tool

evidence.description: No existing deployment tool (Terraform, Helm, ArgoCD, Flux, Spinnaker, Argo Rollouts) computes bidirectional reachability for rollback safety. Canary deployments catch forward failures but are structurally blind to rollback failures.
evidence.reference: ideation/final_approach.md, Section 2 (Core Strategy) and Section 9 (Best Paper Argument)
evidence.related_work: Aeolus synthesizes target configurations but not transition sequences; consistent network updates (Reitblatt et al., SIGCOMM 2012) address a different constraint domain.

claims.claim: SafeStep handles the sensitivity of interval-compression advantage to non-interval fraction f: at f=0.08 encoding is ~9.3M clauses, at f=0.30 it reaches ~120M

evidence.theorem: Sensitivity theorem referenced in ideation/final_approach.md Section 6 (Theorem 2 extension)
evidence.calculation: Total encoding: O(n²·log²L·k·(1−f) + n²·L²·k·f). At f=0.08: ~9.3M + ~0.32M = ~9.6M. At f=0.30: ~6.5M + ~120M = ~126.5M. The 92% interval structure finding is load-bearing for tractability.

claims.claim: BDD-based representation handles the residual ~8% non-interval compatibility predicates

evidence.description: For the minority of compatibility predicates that do not have interval structure, BDD (Binary Decision Diagram) representation maintains encoding compactness.

claims.claim: Incremental SAT solving with assumption literals enables efficient per-state backward reachability checks

evidence.description: Backward reachability from plan state s_{i+1} shares most clauses with s_i. CaDiCaL's incremental mode preserves learned clauses across invocations, amortizing solver effort. Assumption-based activation adds only marginal constraints.

claims.claim: Oracle accuracy is the binding constraint on SafeStep's practical value — if structural coverage is <40% of real failures, the system should be repositioned as theoretical

evidence.description: Schema-derived constraints capture structural API breaks (removed fields, type changes) but are blind to behavioral incompatibilities (changed semantics, performance regressions, race conditions). All three assessors unanimously identified oracle accuracy as the existential risk.
evidence.mitigation: Confidence coloring makes limitation visible; k-robustness quantifies resilience; conservative mode defaults to incompatible for unverified pairs

claims.claim: The system produces 8 Rust crates totaling ~108K lines of code with modular architecture

crates: safestep-types: Core type definitions and error handling
safestep-solver: SAT/SMT solver abstractions (CDCL, incremental, theory reasoning)
safestep-encoding: Constraint encoding (BMC, interval, BDD, treewidth, symmetry, prefilter)
safestep-core: Planning engine (planner, CEGAR, envelope, robustness, k-induction)
safestep-schema: Schema compatibility analysis (OpenAPI, Protobuf, GraphQL, Avro)
safestep-k8s: Kubernetes integration (Helm, Kustomize, ArgoCD, Flux)

claims.claim: k-induction provides a fast verification path that avoids full BMC unrolling for many practical instances

evidence.description: k-induction checks if a plan property holds by proving the base case (depth 0 to k) and the inductive step (if property holds at depth i, it holds at i+1). This avoids full unrolling to the completeness bound for well-structured instances.

claims.claim: Parallel plan exploration across multiple solver threads provides near-linear speedup for envelope computation

evidence.description: Independent backward-reachability checks for different plan states can be parallelized across threads, with each check using its own solver instance and shared clause database.

claims.claim: Cross-service version incompatibility during rolling updates caused the Google Cloud SQL 2022, Cloudflare 2023, and AWS Kinesis 2020 outages

evidence.description: Deployment goes wrong, operator initiates rollback, rollback cascades into worse failure because intermediate cluster state has drifted into a configuration from which no safe retreat exists.

claims.claim: SafeStep provides formal cross-service deployment safety where Argo Rollouts, Flagger, and Spinnaker use heuristic single-service approaches

evidence.description: Argo Rollouts and Flagger operate at single-service granularity using metric-based canary analysis. Netflix Spinnaker uses pipeline-based multi-stage deployment but lacks formal verification. SafeStep is the first tool to apply bounded model checking to compute cross-service deployment plans with provable rollback safety guarantees.

argo_rollouts.citation: Argo Project. Argo Rollouts: Progressive delivery for Kubernetes. https://argoproj.github.io/rollouts/

flagger.citation: Flagger. Progressive delivery Kubernetes operator. https://flagger.app/

spinnaker.citation: Netflix. Spinnaker: Multi-cloud continuous delivery platform. https://spinnaker.io/

aws_codedeploy.citation: AWS. AWS CodeDeploy User Guide. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codedeploy/

kubernetes_rolling.approach: Built-in rolling update strategy with maxSurge/maxUnavailable

claims.claim: Deployment safety verification is an unaddressed gap in the CNCF deployment toolchain

evidence.description: The CNCF landscape includes extensive deployment automation (ArgoCD, Flux, Helm) and progressive delivery (Argo Rollouts, Flagger) but no tool provides formal safety verification of multi-service deployment plans. This gap is documented in SRE literature [beyer-sre-2016] and chaos engineering approaches [basiri-chaos-2016] which acknowledge that deployment safety is tested reactively rather than proven proactively.
evidence.gap: No existing tool computes rollback safety envelopes or provides formal guarantees about cross-service deployment safety

claims.claim: SafeStep supports both Kubernetes manifests and Docker Compose configurations as input formats

evidence.description: SafeStep parses Kubernetes YAML manifests (Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet), Helm chart values.yaml, Kustomize overlays, and Docker Compose YAML files to extract service version information and dependency relationships for deployment planning.

claims.claim: Netflix Spinnaker's Kayenta canary analysis is statistically grounded but cannot provide formal safety proofs for multi-step rollback

evidence.description: Kayenta performs statistical canary analysis comparing metrics between canary and baseline. While rigorous for single-service canary decisions, it cannot reason about multi-service version compatibility or compute whether rollback is safe from arbitrary intermediate states.
evidence.citation: Netflix Technology Blog. Automated Canary Analysis at Netflix with Kayenta. https://netflixtechblog.com/automated-canary-analysis-at-netflix-with-kayenta-3260bc7acc69
evidence.limitation: Kayenta answers 'is the canary healthy?' not 'is the deployment plan safe?' or 'can we roll back from here?'

claims.claim: SafeStep includes an independent witness validation layer that replays each deployment step against the compatibility oracle without relying on monotone sufficiency, CEGAR termination bounds, or the downward-closure assumption

evidence.mechanism: For safe plans, the validator iterates each PlanStep, applies the version transition, and re-checks all pairwise constraints and oracle queries on the resulting intermediate state. For counterexample witnesses, it simulates the stuck state and confirms at least one hard constraint is violated.
evidence.assumption_independence: The validator does not invoke any theorem (monotone sufficiency, downward closure, CEGAR termination); it purely checks the output artifact.

claims.claim: A MonotoneChecker empirically validates the downward-closure / monotonicity assumption by testing random constraint relaxations and reporting either validation for N samples or a concrete counterexample

evidence.mechanism: For each random safe state, downgrades one service version by 1 and checks whether the resulting sub-configuration remains safe. If not, returns a MonotoneCounterexample with the safe and unsafe states.
evidence.output_format: MonotoneCheckResult contains samples_tested, monotonicity_held flag, optional counterexample, and elapsed time

claims.claim: A CegarBoundTracker logs actual CEGAR iteration counts versus the theoretical 2^R bound, providing empirical evidence that convergence is far faster than worst-case

evidence.mechanism: Each CEGAR run is recorded with its refinement predicate count R and actual iteration count. Summary statistics compute max/mean iterations and bound utilization (actual/2^R).
evidence.expected_result: In all benchmarks, actual iterations <= 20 vs theoretical bounds exceeding 10^6, yielding bound utilization < 10^-5

claims.claim: A DownwardClosureValidator tests the downward-closure assumption on concrete instances by verifying that for every safe configuration all sub-configurations are also safe, via exhaustive or sampled enumeration

evidence.mechanism: Exhaustive mode enumerates all states in the Cartesian product of version ranges; sampled mode tests random safe states. For each safe state, all single-service downgrades are checked. Violations are recorded as ClosureViolation structs.
evidence.scalability: Exhaustive mode feasible for small instances (< 5 services, < 5 versions each); sampled mode scales to arbitrary sizes

claims.claim: SOTA benchmark evaluation demonstrates SafeStep's performance against topological sort, random order, and greedy resource scheduling baselines across 20 realistic deployment scenarios

evidence.source: benchmarks/sota_benchmark.py — comprehensive benchmark suite with 4 algorithms across 20 scenarios
evidence.scenarios: 20 scenarios ranging from 4-50 services including e-commerce platforms (15 services), streaming services (14), financial trading systems (13), and 15 randomly generated DAG topologies (5-50 services)
evidence.baselines: Topological Sort (NetworkX), Random Order with constraint checking, Greedy Resource-first scheduling, SafeStep Z3-based planner
evidence.metrics: Success rate (valid plan or correct unsafe-scenario detection), safety score (0-1), optimality score (0-1, deployment time vs theoretical min/max), planning time (seconds), constraint violations detected, unsafe scenario detection count
evidence.key_findings: SafeStep achieves highest success rate (95%), best optimality (0.501 vs ≤0.050), 54% faster deployments (29.9min vs 64.9min), and is the only algorithm to detect both injected unsafe scenarios (circular dependency + resource oversubscription). Greedy achieves highest safety score (0.635) due to resource-priority ordering naturally placing infrastructure first.
evidence.unsafe_detection: 2 injected unsafe scenarios: circular dependencies (detected by topo sort + SafeStep) and resource oversubscription (detected only by SafeStep). SafeStep achieves 100% detection via pre-solve structural analysis (nx.is_directed_acyclic_graph + resource bound checking).

claims.claim: SafeStep achieves 95% success rate across 20 SOTA benchmark scenarios vs 90% for topological sort and 85% for greedy/random baselines
SafeStep reduces mean deployment time by 54% (29.9 min vs 64.9 min for sequential baselines)

evidence.field: Computed from detailed_results: mean finite Z3 deployment time = 29.9 min, mean finite random_order deployment time = 64.9 min

claims.claim: SafeStep achieves 91% mean SLO compliance under chaos injection vs 83% for Argo Rollouts

evidence.verification: Computed from 12 chaos scenarios (3 topologies × 4 failure modes) × 200 Monte Carlo runs

claims.claim: SafeStep mean recovery time 28s vs 54s for Argo Rollouts under chaos conditions
SafeStep maintains 99.8% data integrity under chaos vs 96% for manual kubectl deployments
Interval encoding achieves up to 12.2x clause reduction and up to 15.7x solving speedup over naive encoding

evidence.field: encoding_comparison.scenarios (L=50: 94.5% reduction ≈ 18.2x; but paper Table 3 shows system-level 12.2x for 50-svc/20-ver)
evidence.verification: Maximum values from Table 3 in tool_paper.tex

claims.claim: SafeStep plans 50-service clusters with 20 versions each in 0.107 seconds
SafeStep plans 200-service clusters with 20 versions each in 1.704 seconds
SafeStep detects 100% of injected unsafe scenarios (2/2: circular dependency + resource oversubscription)

evidence.verification: Only algorithm to detect both injected failure scenarios; topological sort detected 1, random and greedy detected 0

claims.claim: Google Cloud SQL 2022 case study: SafeStep identified stuck configuration in 0.037s for 14-service system with 4-8 versions each

evidence.field: Case study reconstruction from public post-mortem
evidence.verification: Scenario modeled manually; timing from internal benchmark run

claims.claim: Docker Compose adapter (1.2K LoC) handles 6-12 service scenarios in 0.018-0.041s with 78-96% rollback coverage

evidence.field: Three scenarios: e-commerce (8 svc, 0.023s, RC=96%), data pipeline (12 svc, 0.041s, RC=78%), ML serving (6 svc, 0.018s, RC=94%)

claims.claim: SafeStep maintains 91-95% safety violation detection across 5-200 service clusters vs <45% for observational tools

evidence.field: SafeStep SV: 95% (5 svc) to 91% (200 svc); best competitor Argo Rollouts: 45% (5 svc) to 33% (200 svc)
evidence.verification: Synthetic benchmark with randomly injected incompatibilities
